headline: Sabah election: PH wins 1 of 22, DAP wiped out for first time since 2004
context: The 17th Sabah state election on 29 November 2025 returned GRS to power and reduced Pakatan Harapan to 1 of 22 contested seats. DAP lost all 8 it ran in — its first total absence from the assembly since 2004. Hajiji Noor was sworn in for a second term without a simple majority.

cards.big: The 17th Sabah state election on 29 November 2025 returned GRS to power and reduced Pakatan Harapan to one of 22 contested seats.
cards.sub: GRS chairman Hajiji Noor was sworn in for a second term despite winning only 29 of 73 seats — 8 short of a simple majority.

cards.big: DAP contested 8 seats and won zero — its first total absence from the Sabah assembly since 2004.
cards.sub: PKR took PH's lone seat. Of DAP's 6 previously held seats, all were lost. PH contested 22 of 73 seats statewide.

cards.big: All five Chinese-majority seats — Luyang, Likas, Api-Api, Elopura, Sri Tanjung — swung from DAP and PKR to Warisan.
cards.sub: Analysts called it a 'Chinese tsunami' against the federal coalition. Urban turnout (49-56%) trailed the 64.4% statewide average.

cards.big: Hajiji obtained dissolution on 6 October 2025; nomination 15 November; polling 29 November. GRS won 29 of 73 seats — coalition needed.
cards.sub: 37 was the simple-majority line. GRS formed government with smaller parties; the 'Sabah First' narrative outperformed federal-affiliation appeals.

cards.big: It was not the federal opposition that took DAP's Chinese seats. It was a local party with no Peninsular brand.
Sabah voters returned a fractured map and a 'Sabah First' verdict. The 29-seat GRS plurality holds because alternatives fragmented, not because the mandate is wide.
